title: The Dummy Variable Trap
concept: The dummy variable trap occurs when one-hot encoded columns are perfectly predictable from each other, causing multi-collinearity issues.

exercises.prompt: Warmup: What is the main drawback of using one-hot encoding on a feature with many unique categories?

options.text: It creates a massive number of new columns
It loses the ordering of the categories
It converts numerical features into text

exercises.explain: One-hot encoding creates a new column for every single unique category, which can quickly bloat your dataset size.

cards.title: The redundant coin flip
cards.body: If you flip a coin and it isn't heads, it must be tails. You don't need a column for both is_heads and is_tails — knowing one tells you the other instantly.

cards.title: The Dummy Variable Trap
cards.body: The dummy variable trap is a scenario where one-hot encoded columns are perfectly predictable from one another, causing a mathematical issue called multi-collinearity.

cards.title: The two-way light switch
cards.body: Imagine a room with a single switch. It's either UP (on) or DOWN (off). You don't need two separate switches next to each other that always show opposite states.

cards.title: Gender encoding
cards.body: When encoding 'Male' and 'Female', if is_male is 1, is_female is 0. If is_male is 0, is_female is 1. One column is redundant, as is_female always equals 1 - is_male.

cards.title: Always drop one column
cards.body: You don't need all columns! To avoid the trap, always drop one of the encoded columns (setting drop_first=True in libraries). If you have N categories, you only need N-1 columns.

cards.title: Before and after dropping
cards.body: How dropping the redundant column works:

exercises.prompt: What is multi-collinearity in machine learning datasets?

options.text: When one or more features can be perfectly predicted from other features
When a dataset has too many missing values
When the target variable is numerical instead of categorical

exercises.explain: Multi-collinearity occurs when independent variables are highly correlated, confusing algorithms like linear regression.

exercises.prompt: True or false: To avoid the dummy variable trap, you should keep only N-1 columns when one-hot encoding a feature with N categories.
exercises.explain: True. Dropping the first column removes the redundant correlation without losing any information.

exercises.sentence: To avoid the dummy variable trap, you should ___ one of the encoded columns.
exercises.explain: Dropping one column prevents perfect predictability among the features.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following sets of columns has a redundant dummy variable trap for a category with values: [Yes, No]?
exercises.explain: Having both columns is redundant because if is_yes is 1, is_no must be 0, and vice versa.

exercises.prompt: You are predicting home prices using a model. You one-hot encode a 'heating_type' column with three options: 'Gas', 'Electric', and 'Solar'. If you drop the 'Gas' column to avoid the trap, how does the model know a house has gas heating?

options.text: When both is_electric and is_solar are 0
The model will completely lose the information about gas heating
The model will crash due to missing columns
You have to add a separate gas column back in

exercises.explain: Correct! If the house doesn't use Electric or Solar, it must use Gas. The information is preserved perfectly in the remaining columns. Next up: how we handle categories with hundreds or thousands of options!
